flashcards.front: What is a multithreading model in operating systems?
flashcards.back: A multithreading model defines how user threads (created by applications) are mapped to kernel threads (managed by the OS). It determines how thread scheduling and management are handled between user space and kernel space.

In the Many-to-One model, multiple user threads are mapped to a single kernel thread. All thread management is done in user space by a thread library. If a thread makes a blocking system call, the entire process blocks.

flashcards.front: What is the One-to-One multithreading model?
flashcards.back: The One-to-One model maps each user thread to a separate kernel thread. It allows true parallelism as kernel can schedule threads on multiple processors. The main drawback is that creating many threads can burden the kernel.

The Many-to-Many model multiplexes many user threads to an equal or lesser number of kernel threads. It combines the benefits of both models: user can create any number of threads and kernel can schedule them on multiple processors.

flashcards.front: What is a Two-level multithreading model?
flashcards.back: The Two-level model is a variation of Many-to-Many where some user threads are bound to a single kernel thread (one-to-one) while others remain multiplexed (many-to-many). It provides more control for certain applications.

flashcards.front: Define User Threads and Kernel Threads.
flashcards.back: User threads are threads managed entirely in user space by a thread library without OS support. Kernel threads are threads managed directly by the operating system kernel. The multithreading model defines how these two relate.

flashcards.front: What are the benefits of multithreading?
flashcards.back: Benefits include: enhanced responsiveness (GUI remains interactive), resource sharing (threads share memory/files), economy (creating threads is cheaper than processes), and utilization of multiprocessor architectures for true parallelism.

flashcards.front: What is thread scalability in multithreading?
flashcards.back: Thread scalability refers to how well an application performs as the number of threads increases. Good scalability means performance continues to improve with more threads, limited by available CPU cores.

flashcards.front: What issue arises when using fork() in multithreaded programs?
flashcards.address: In a multithreaded program, fork() creates a process with only the calling thread. If other threads held locks, the child process may deadlock when trying to acquire those locks since they are not duplicated.

flashcards.front: Define thread cancellation in operating systems.
flashcards.back: Thread cancellation is the task of terminating a thread before it completes. Types include: asynchronous cancellation (immediate termination) and deferred cancellation (thread checks cancellation point periodically).

Thread pooling is a technique where a fixed number of worker threads are created in advance to perform tasks. Tasks are submitted to a queue and executed by available threads, avoiding the overhead of creating new threads for each task.

flashcards.front: How does the Many-to-One model handle blocking system calls?
flashcards.back: Since all user threads map to one kernel thread, if any thread makes a blocking system call, the kernel blocks that single kernel thread, causing the entire process to block even though other user threads exist.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the thread library in multithreading?
flashcards.back: The thread library provides APIs for creating, managing, and synchronizing threads. In user-level threading (Many-to-One), it handles all thread management. Popular libraries include POSIX Pthreads, Java threads, and Windows Threads.

flashcards.front: Why is the One-to-One model more scalable than Many-to-One?
flashcards.back: The One-to-One model allows kernel to schedule different threads on multiple CPUs simultaneously, providing true parallelism. The Many-to-One cannot achieve parallelism because only one kernel thread exists for all user threads.

flashcards.front: What is thread synchronization and why is it needed?
flashcards.back: Thread synchronization coordinates access to shared resources among concurrent threads. It is needed to prevent race conditions, data inconsistency, and ensure thread-safe access to shared memory, files, or other resources.
